ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers soya bean curd products broadly; no specific code for fuzhu salad as a dressed cold dish. Used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for fuzhu salad or beancurd skin salad as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard side dish portion of fuzhu salad is approximately 200 g, typically served as an accompaniment to a rice bowl or bento set.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Tofu, raw (FDC ID 172444) and Mushrooms, wood ear, dried (FDC ID 168436); adapted composite estimate for fuzhu salad
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for fuzhu salad exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from a composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for tofu skin / yuba (FDC ID 172444 and related entries), wood ear mushroom, and typical Chinese cold-dressed salad dressing components (soy sauce, sesame oil, vinegar). Input values from the source record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against USDA component data and retained where plausible. Energy 90 kcal, protein 6 g, fat 5 g, carbohydrate 5 g per 100 g are consistent with a tofu-skin-dominant cold salad. Sodium 225 mg/100 g reflects soy sauce dressing contribution. Values are flagged as estimated.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Tofu / soy-based foods analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fuzhu salad. GI estimated at 32 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for tofu and soy-based foods (GI ~32, low category), which is the dominant macronutrient contributor in this dish. Carbohydrate content is low (5 g/100 g), predominantly from vegetables and small amounts of dressing sugar. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (32 × 10 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.2, classified as low GL. Vinegar in the dressing is documented in peer-reviewed literature to further attenuate postprandial glycaemic response.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 225 mg per 100 g from soy sauce dressing
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce in the dressing to lower sodium below 200 mg/100 g; the dish's potassium contribution from carrot, cucumber, and black fungus provides partial offset, and sesame oil contributes cardioprotective unsaturated fats.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5 g/100 g), low GI (~32), and moderate protein (6 g/100 g) from fuzhu
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fuzhu salad is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycaemic index (~32), low carbohydrate load (GL 3.2 per 200 g serving), and moderate soy protein content; vinegar in the dressing has evidence-based properties for attenuating postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.75 g/100 g), zero trans fat, and soy isoflavones from fuzhu
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fuzhu salad is broadly suitable for cardiovascular health given its low saturated fat content (0.75 g/100 g), absence of trans fat, and soy isoflavones from beancurd skin which have evidence-based LDL-lowering properties; sodium at 225 mg/100 g should be moderated by requesting reduced soy sauce.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory components (black fungus beta-glucans, sesame oil polyphenols, vinegar acetic acid) and low saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fuzhu salad is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory ingredient profile; individuals sensitive to nightshades should request omission of chilli oil, and reduced soy sauce is advisable to limit sodium-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fuzhu Salad (腐竹沙拉; also known as Liang Ban Fuzhu or Cold Beancurd Skin Salad) is a refreshing Chinese cold salad in which fuzhu — dried beancurd skin or tofu skin (yuba) — serves as the primary ingredient. The fuzhu is rehydrated and blanched until tender, then combined with julienned carrot, sliced cucumber, and reconstituted black fungus (wood ear mushroom). The salad is dressed with a mixture of soy sauce, rice vinegar, sesame oil, minced garlic, and optionally chilli oil and fresh coriander. The dish is served cold or at room temperature and is commonly offered as a side dish in Singaporean Chinese rice bowl and bento sets. Texturally, fuzhu is silky and slightly chewy, with a mild soy flavour that absorbs the tangy, savoury dressing well. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 90 kcal per 100 g, with 6 g protein, 5 g fat, and 5 g carbohydrate. Its low glycaemic index (~32) and low glycaemic load (GL 3.2 per 200 g serving) make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Saturated fat is low at 0.75 g/100 g and trans fat is absent, supporting cardiovascular health. Soy isoflavones in fuzhu have documented mild LDL-lowering properties. Sodium at 225 mg/100 g is the primary clinical caution for hypertension and rheumatoid arthritis patients, and reduced soy sauce is recommended. The dish is naturally vegetarian, vegan, and dairy-free, and is a culturally significant cold appetiser in Chinese cuisine across Singapore and Southeast Asia.

Nutrient values are estimated from a composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for tofu skin, wood ear mushroom, and typical Chinese cold-dressing components. Input values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against USDA component data and retained where plausible. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for tofu and soy-based foods. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central entries for tofu skin / yuba, wood ear mushroom, and typical soy-sauce-vinegar-sesame dressing components. No exact approved-source entry for fuzhu salad as a prepared dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. Input values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were retained where consistent with USDA component data. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this composite Chinese cold salad preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): fuzhu and vegetable-based cold salad with sesame oil and soy sauce dressing contains no hydrogenated fats; trans fat is naturally zero by ingredient composition. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): all ingredients are plant-based; cholesterol is naturally zero. 4. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for tofu and soy-based foods; no direct GI measurement for fuzhu salad exists in the approved GI literature. 5. Ontology codes are adapted matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code for fuzhu salad as a prepared cold dish was identified. 6. The dish is classified as Singaporean Chinese for multilingual naming purposes, requiring English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi coverage — all satisfied.
